subsystem: Poppler (xpdf-derived) PDF rendering library — the font-enumeration pass. FontInfoScanner::scanFonts walks a PDF page/form resource dictionary, builds a GfxFontDict of the fonts declared under the /Font key, records each distinct font into a caller-supplied list, and then recursively descends into the /Resources dictionaries reachable through /XObject and /Pattern entries so that fonts used only inside form XObjects or tiling/shading patterns are also discovered.
provenance: Identified by the Poppler/xpdf type vocabulary and method names: FontInfoScanner::scanFonts, GfxFontDict, GfxFont, FontInfo, and the Object API (XRef*, Dict*, Object::lookupNF, Object::fetch, Object::isRef/isDict/isStream, Object::getRef, Object::getDict, Object::dictGetLength, Object::dictGetValNF, Object::streamGetDict) are all Poppler classes. The file banner is corpus/train/train_0014_cwe674.cpp and the leading comment is '/* sample: primevul_train_2369_vuln */'. No SPDX/copyright line is present in the excerpt; identification rests on the API surface and class names.
trust_boundary: The two Object graphs reached through resDict and xrefA originate entirely from an untrusted PDF document. An attacker who supplies the PDF controls the shape of the object graph: which entries exist under /Font, /XObject and /Pattern, whether each entry is an indirect reference or an inline object, whether a referenced object resolves to a stream, and — critically — how deeply /Resources dictionaries nest inside one another and whether they form chains or cycles. resDict, the fetched obj2 objects, the /Resources sub-dictionaries, and every Ref number are therefore all hostile. The function is reached during ordinary font inventory (e.g. pdffonts, or any consumer walking a document's fonts), so a single crafted file is enough to drive it.

what_the_code_does.explanation: scanFonts is a member of FontInfoScanner. It takes the document cross-reference table xrefA (used to resolve indirect references to concrete objects), resDict — a resource dictionary drawn from the PDF whose fonts are to be enumerated — and fontsList, an out-parameter vector into which newly discovered FontInfo objects are appended. Two locals are declared: gfxFontDict, which will hold the parsed font dictionary for this level, and font, a scratch pointer reused inside the loop. Because scanFonts calls itself further down (line 55), it is a recursive traversal keyed on the untrusted resource-dictionary graph.

gfxFontDict is initialised to nullptr so the later 'if (gfxFontDict)' guard is well-defined even when no font dictionary is found. lookupNF("Font") retrieves the value of the /Font key WITHOUT following (Not Following) an indirect reference, returning a const reference to the stored Object. Thus fontObj may itself be a raw indirect reference (Ref) rather than the resolved dictionary; the code disambiguates that on the next lines.

If /Font is an indirect reference, fetch(xrefA) resolves it to the actual object obj2. Only when obj2 is a dictionary does the code proceed: it recovers the reference r (so GfxFontDict can attribute font sub-objects to the correct owning object number) and constructs a new GfxFontDict on the heap from the resolved dictionary obj2.getDict(). The reference address &r is safe to pass because GfxFontDict copies what it needs during construction while r is still in scope.

The alternative branch handles a /Font value that is an inline (direct) dictionary rather than a reference. In that case there is no owning Ref, so nullptr is passed for the ref argument and the dictionary is taken directly. After this block gfxFontDict is either a freshly allocated GfxFontDict or still nullptr.

When a font dictionary was built, the code iterates every font it contains via getNumFonts()/getFont(i). getFont(i) may return nullptr for a slot that failed to parse, so its result is assigned and null-checked in one expression. For a valid font it reads the font's identity by dereferencing font->getID(): getID() returns a pointer to the font's Ref, and '*font->getID()' copies that Ref (object/generation number) into fontRef, which is used as the dedup key.

'fonts' is a FontInfoScanner member set that remembers the object numbers of fonts already reported across the whole scan. If this font's object number is not present, a new FontInfo is heap-allocated (wrapping the GfxFont and xref), appended to the caller's fontsList, and the number is inserted into the dedup set so the same font is not reported twice even when it is referenced from multiple resource dictionaries. Ownership of the new FontInfo is transferred to the caller via fontsList.

After the font loop the GfxFontDict allocated at line 13 or 16 is released with a scalar delete, matching its scalar new — no array/scalar mismatch. The delete is inside the 'if (gfxFontDict)' block so it only runs when a dictionary was actually allocated; the nullptr case skips it.

The second half descends into nested resources. resTypes is a fixed 2-element array of the resource categories whose members can themselves carry a /Resources dictionary: form XObjects and patterns. The outer loop is bounded by sizeof(resTypes)/sizeof(resTypes[0]) (=2). For each category it looks up the sub-dictionary (this time WITH reference resolution, via lookup) and, if it is a dictionary, iterates its entries by index using dictGetLength()/dictGetValNF(i). dictGetValNF returns the entry value without resolving references, so dictObjI may be a Ref.

This is the cycle-avoidance guard. When the resource entry is an indirect reference, its object number r.num is looked up in the 'visitedObjects' member set. If already present, the entry is skipped (continue) so the same object is not descended into twice; otherwise the number is recorded. Because visitedObjects is a member and persists across the recursive calls, this prevents infinite loops caused by resource objects that reference one another cyclically THROUGH indirect references. Note the guard runs only for reference entries; inline entries bypass it, but those cannot form indirect cycles.

The entry is resolved to obj2. Form XObjects and patterns are stream objects, so only when obj2 is a stream does the code look at its stream dictionary and fetch that stream's own /Resources dictionary. If /Resources is a dictionary AND is not literally the same Dict pointer as the current resDict (a one-level self-recursion guard), scanFonts recurses into it. This is the recursion edge: the depth of the call stack equals the nesting depth of /Resources dictionaries an attacker chains together in the document.

Closing braces for the inner entry loop, the isDict guard, the resType loop, and the function. Control returns to the caller (or the parent recursion frame) after both the current level's fonts and all reachable nested resource dictionaries have been scanned.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if (resObj.isDict() && resObj.getDict() != resDict) {
            scanFonts(xrefA, resObj.getDict(), fontsList);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: scanFonts recurses into the /Resources dictionary of every form XObject and pattern with NO recursion-depth limit. The only guards are (1) visitedObjects, which skips REFERENCED resource objects already seen, and (2) the pointer inequality resObj.getDict() != resDict, which blocks only immediate one-level self-recursion. Neither bounds the DEPTH of the recursion: a crafted PDF can chain thousands of distinct stream objects, each whose /Resources references the next distinct object (a linear chain O1->O2->...->On of unique object numbers). Every hop passes the visitedObjects check (each number is new) and the != resDict check (each dict differs), so the traversal recurses once per object and the C++ call stack grows one frame per link. With enough links the process runs out of stack and crashes — an attacker-controlled, remote denial of service from merely enumerating a document's fonts. Invariant the implementation must uphold: cap the recursion depth (or convert to an explicit bounded worklist) so document-controlled nesting cannot exhaust the stack; visited-object de-duplication alone does not provide that bound.

The cycle guard is keyed only on r.num (object number, ignoring generation) and — more importantly — is populated ONLY inside 'if (dictObjI.isRef())'. The recursion target at line 55 is resObj.getDict(), the /Resources dictionary, which is not what gets inserted; visitedObjects tracks the enclosing XObject/Pattern stream object, not the Resources dict being entered. This is sufficient to stop indirect-reference cycles from looping forever, but it silently does nothing to bound stack depth (see the primary finding) and provides no protection at all for a resource sub-dictionary reached as an INLINE object rather than a reference. The invariant the implementation must uphold is that the recursion terminates for every attacker-shaped graph and within a bounded stack; a visited-set that prevents revisiting nodes does not by itself prevent unbounded depth.

font is null-checked, but the value returned by font->getID() is not. In Poppler GfxFont::getID() returns a pointer to the font's Ref that can be null for a font that has no associated object id. Dereferencing it with '*font->getID()' would then read through a null pointer and crash. An attacker who can cause GfxFontDict to build a GfxFont whose id pointer is null turns this into a null-pointer dereference denial of service. Invariant: the id pointer must be validated (or guaranteed non-null by GfxFontDict) before dereference.

obj2.isStream() has been confirmed, so streamGetDict() is expected to return the stream's dictionary, but the returned pointer is dereferenced immediately with ->lookup("Resources") without a null check. If any malformed-stream path can yield a stream whose dictionary accessor returns null, this dereference crashes. Lower likelihood than the recursion issue because a valid stream carries a dictionary, but it is an unguarded dereference on an untrusted-object path worth confirming against the Object/Stream implementation.

summary: This is Poppler's FontInfoScanner::scanFonts, a recursive walk of untrusted PDF resource dictionaries. Its dominant risk is uncontrolled recursion (CWE-674): the descent into nested /XObject and /Pattern /Resources dictionaries has no depth cap, and the visitedObjects/!=resDict guards prevent cycles but not depth, so a crafted chain of nested resources exhausts the stack and crashes the process (remote DoS). Secondary latent crashes exist at the unchecked '*font->getID()' dereference (CWE-476) and the unchecked streamGetDict() dereference.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Self-recursive traversal of an untrusted PDF object graph with no depth bound — uncontrolled recursion.
Reference-preserving dictionary lookup; result type is disambiguated by isRef/isDict below. No size or length contract.
Resolves an indirect reference; result is type-checked (isDict) before use.
Scalar new matched by the scalar delete at line 30; &r is consumed during construction while r is in scope.
Inline-dictionary construction path; same matched new/delete pairing, no ref available so nullptr is correct.
Loop bound comes from the parsed font dictionary; index i is used only through getFont(i) which is null-checked.
Return value is null-checked in the if condition before use.
getID() pointer is dereferenced without a null check — latent NULL dereference.
Membership set keyed on object number; deduplication only, no buffer or size contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: new FontInfo(font, xrefA) pushed to fontsList (line 25)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Heap object ownership is transferred to the caller via fontsList by design; not freed here — a leak only if the caller mismanages it, which is outside this contract.
Scalar delete correctly paired with scalar new; guarded by the non-null check.
Fixed 2-element array of string literals; iteration bound derived from sizeof, no attacker influence.
Compile-time constant bound (2); no overflow surface.
Reference-resolving lookup; result is type-checked (isDict) before iteration.
Index bounded by the dictionary's own length; dictGetValNF returns a reference-preserving Object handled below.
Cycle guard prevents revisiting referenced objects but does not bound recursion depth and is not populated with the recursion target dict — part of the uncontrolled-recursion contract.
Resolves an indirect reference; result is type-checked (isStream) before use.
streamGetDict() pointer dereferenced without a null check on an untrusted-stream path.
One-level self-recursion guard only; the recursive scanFonts call has no depth bound — the core uncontrolled-recursion anchor.

findings.title: Uncontrolled recursion over nested PDF resource dictionaries (stack exhaustion DoS)
findings.anchor: scanFonts(xrefA, resObj.getDict(), fontsList) with only visitedObjects and '!= resDict' as guards
findings.code:
if (resObj.isDict() && resObj.getDict() != resDict) {
            scanFonts(xrefA, resObj.getDict(), fontsList);
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion (no depth limit)
findings.invariant: The traversal of document-controlled /Resources nesting must terminate within a bounded call-stack depth — e.g. an explicit recursion-depth cap or an iterative worklist — regardless of how the attacker chains XObject/Pattern resources. Cycle de-duplication (visitedObjects) prevents revisiting nodes but does NOT bound depth.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted PDF builds a chain of distinct stream objects O1->O2->...->On where each object's /Resources references the next unique object. Each hop presents a new object number (passing the visitedObjects check) and a different Dict pointer (passing '!= resDict'), so scanFonts recurses once per link, adding a stack frame each time. With a sufficiently long chain the process exhausts its stack and crashes — a remote, attacker-controlled denial of service triggered merely by enumerating the document's fonts.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no enclosing depth counter or recursion limit around scanFonts in the FontInfoScanner implementation, and that visitedObjects is the only loop guard; verify whether an upstream cap on nesting depth exists elsewhere in the call chain.

findings.title: NULL-pointer dereference of font->getID()
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0014_cwe674.cpp (line 21), with GfxFont::getID() in the implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The pointer returned by GfxFont::getID() must be non-null before it is dereferenced; either the font is guaranteed by GfxFontDict to carry a valid id, or the call site must null-check it.
findings.failure_mode: font is null-checked but font->getID() is not. If a parsed font legitimately has no id (getID() returns null), '*font->getID()' reads through a null pointer and crashes the process — a denial of service driven by a font entry in an attacker-supplied PDF.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether GfxFont::getID() can return nullptr (fonts constructed without a Ref) and whether GfxFontDict guarantees a non-null id for every entry returned by getFont().

findings.title: Unchecked streamGetDict() dereference on untrusted stream
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0014_cwe674.cpp (line 53), with Object::streamGetDict() in the implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: streamGetDict() must return a valid dictionary pointer for any object for which isStream() is true; otherwise the call site must null-check before dereferencing.
findings.failure_mode: isStream() is verified, then streamGetDict() is dereferenced immediately with ->lookup("Resources") with no null check. If any malformed-stream path yields a stream whose dictionary accessor returns null, this crashes. Likelihood is low because a valid stream carries a dictionary, but it is an unguarded dereference on an attacker-influenced object.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that Object::streamGetDict() cannot return null for an object that passes isStream() in the Poppler Object/Stream implementation.

audit_checklist: Add or verify a recursion-depth cap (or convert to an explicit bounded stack/worklist) around scanFonts so document-controlled /Resources nesting cannot exhaust the process stack.
Confirm visitedObjects persists for the whole scan and reason about whether depth — not just node de-duplication — is actually bounded for adversarial linear chains of distinct objects.
Null-check font->getID() before dereferencing at line 21, or prove GfxFontDict never returns a font with a null id.
Null-check obj2.streamGetDict() before ->lookup("Resources") at line 53, or prove it is non-null whenever isStream() is true.
Verify the new/delete pairing for GfxFontDict (lines 13/16 vs 30) and that ownership of each new FontInfo pushed to fontsList (line 25) is cleaned up by the caller.
Consider whether inline (non-reference) XObject/Pattern resource sub-dictionaries can bypass the visitedObjects guard and still drive recursion at line 55.
